state_profiles.agricultural_highlights: Virginia pairs Piedmont horse country with strong poultry and row-crop sectors; eastern shore vegetables and Shenandoah orchards diversify local seasons.
fun_facts: Virginia vies with surrounding states for No. 1 in U.S. apple production depending on the year.
Smithfield ham and Chesapeake oysters tie coastal Virginia to iconic seasonal foods.

state_profiles.agricultural_highlights: West Virginia emphasizes livestock, forage, and hillside orchards—small farms keep strong direct-to-consumer markets.
fun_facts: Ramp festivals each spring celebrate one of Appalachia’s most distinctive wild edibles.
Poultry remains one of the state’s largest agricultural commodities by value.

state_profiles.agricultural_highlights: North Carolina leads U.S. sweet potato production and is a major broiler and hog state; the Research Triangle anchors innovation in plant breeding.
fun_facts: North Carolina routinely harvests more sweet potatoes than any other state.
Peak blueberry and strawberry U-pick seasons draw tourists to coastal and piedmont farms.

state_profiles.agricultural_highlights: South Carolina’s poultry sector and historic peach industry define much of its farm economy, with long warm seasons for fresh-market vegetables.
fun_facts: South Carolina markets itself as the largest fresh peach producer in the eastern U.S.
Low-country rice culture shaped regional cuisine long before today’s row crops.

state_profiles.agricultural_highlights: Georgia dominates U.S. poultry, peanuts, and pecans while blueberries rival traditional orchard crops; Vidalia onions mark an iconic spring harvest.
fun_facts: Georgia is the largest peanut-producing state in the country.
Pecan orchards across south Georgia anchor fall harvest festivals.

state_profiles.agricultural_highlights: Florida’s mild winters make it the nation’s winter vegetable garden—citrus belts shift south after freezes; tropical fruit thrives in south Florida.
fun_facts: Florida produces the majority of U.S. orange juice oranges most years.
The Plant City area is famous for winter–spring strawberry harvests.

state_profiles.agricultural_highlights: Alabama couples intensive poultry houses with catfish ponds, cotton, and peanuts—summer vegetables flood roadside stands.
fun_facts: Alabama remains one of the top broiler-producing states nationally.
Farm-raised catfish from west Alabama is a regional staple.

state_profiles.agricultural_highlights: Mississippi’s Delta is a powerhouse for row crops and aquaculture; sweet potatoes anchor fall storage crops.
fun_facts: Mississippi ranks among the top states for farm-raised catfish.
Long summers push watermelon season earlier than northern markets.

state_profiles.agricultural_highlights: Tennessee spans productive row-crop valleys and rolling pasture—pick-your-own berries and peaches follow spring wheat and hay cycles.
fun_facts: Middle Tennessee once styled itself the 'nursery capital' for ornamentals.
Whiskey-grade corn and heirloom vegetables thrive in small-batch farms.

state_profiles.agricultural_highlights: Kentucky’s limestone pastures define thoroughbred culture, while rolling cropland supports bourbon grains and a rebound in horticulture.
fun_facts: Kentucky leads the nation in burley tobacco acreage historically tied to farm culture.
Small vegetable farms supply vibrant Louisville and Lexington farmers’ markets.

state_profiles.agricultural_highlights: Arkansas often tops U.S. rice production; row-crop counties transition into Ozark poultry and fruit enclaves.
fun_facts: Stuttgart-area rice fields host famous duck-hunting habitat tied to wetland agriculture.
Watermelons from the Arkansas River Valley reach peak sweetness in high summer.

state_profiles.agricultural_highlights: Louisiana marries Gulf seafood culture with sugarcane and rice deltas—crawfish season is a social calendar as much as an agricultural one.
fun_facts: Louisiana leads the nation in crawfish and alligator farming.
Cajun and Creole cuisines tie closely to seasonal shellfish and greens.
